1:
"And in those days, when the number of the disciples was multiplied, there arose a murmuring of the Grecians against the Hebrews, because their widows were neglected in the daily ministration." The witnesses note the first internal discord in the church, arising amid its rapid growth. The puritan Henry observes the irony: "It does our hearts good to find that the number of the disciples is multiplied... Yet it casts a damp upon us to find that the multiplying of the disciples proves an occasion of discord. Hitherto they were all with one accord... but now that they were multiplied, they began to murmur; as in the old world, when men began to multiply, they corrupted themselves."

The grammatical voices identify the parties. The "Grecians" (Hellenists) were Greek-speaking Jews, scattered in the dispersion, who read the Old Testament in the Greek version; the "Hebrews" were the native, Aramaic-speaking Jews. Henry: "their joint-embracing of the faith of Christ did not prevail, as it ought to have done, to extinguish the little jealousies they had one of another before their conversion... not understanding that in Christ Jesus there is neither Greek nor Jew."

The cause of the murmuring the witnesses note as a matter of charity. Henry: "The first contention in the Christian church was about a money-matter... A great deal of money was gathered for the relief of the poor, but, as often happens, it was impossible to please every body in the laying of it out." Whether the complaint was well-founded or not, the Hellenist widows were thought neglected in the daily distribution.

The witnesses agree: amid the church's rapid growth, the first internal discord arose — a murmuring of the Greek-speaking Jews (Hellenists) against the native Hebrews, that their widows were neglected in the daily distribution of charity. The first contention in the church was over a money-matter (Henry), the old national jealousies surviving conversion where they ought to have been extinguished in Christ, in whom there is neither Greek nor Jew. The verse displays both the glory of the church's increase and the danger that growth brings: where numbers multiply, occasions of friction arise. Yet the discord, wisely handled (vv.2-6), issued not in division but in better order and further increase (v.7). The verse marks the realism of the apostolic record: the early church was no idealized community free from tension, but a real fellowship that had to confront and overcome the first stirrings of discord. The apostles' prudent remedy turned a threatened division into an occasion of growth.
2:
"Then the twelve called the multitude of the disciples unto them, and said, It is not reason that we should leave the word of God, and serve tables." The witnesses note the apostles' wise and humble handling of the crisis, and their priority of the word. Ellicott: "The Apostles meet the crisis with singular tact and moderation. They do not resent the suspicion; they are not careful to vindicate themselves against it. They remembered, it may be, the precedent presented by the life of Moses (Exodus 18:25), and they act, as he had acted, by delegating part of their authority to others."

The grammatical voices explain the terms. Barnes: "It is not reason — the meaning evidently is, it is not fit, or proper. It would be a departure from the design of their appointment, which was to preach the gospel, and not to attend to the pecuniary affairs of the church." Robertson: "Serve tables... the tables used in the common daily distribution of the food." The "word of God" is the apostles' primary charge; the administration of charity, however necessary, must not displace it.

The Reformed Calvin draws out both the patience and the principle: "It is a point of patience and meekness that the apostles are no more moved. It is a point of prudence and godly carefulness, in that they prevent the evil which began to arise." And on the priority: "they declare that the ministry of the word is so painful that it requireth a whole man, neither will it suffer him to be occupied about any other business... let every one of us that is called unto the function of teaching addict himself wholly to order this his estate well."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the twelve, meeting the crisis with tact and moderation (Ellicott), called the disciples and declared it not fitting to leave the word of God to serve tables. The principle is the priority of the ministry of the word: the apostles' primary charge was preaching, which (Calvin) requires the whole man and must not be displaced by administration, however necessary. They do not despise the care of the poor — they provide for it (vv.3-6) — but they will not let it crowd out the word. The verse displays both the apostles' humility (delegating authority rather than clinging to it) and their wisdom (preserving their proper work). It teaches the abiding lesson that ministers of the word must guard their primary calling, not allowing even good and necessary business to draw them from prayer and preaching. The apostles, following Moses' precedent, share the burden, that both the word and the widows may be well served.
3:
"Wherefore, brethren, look ye out among you seven men of honest report, full of the Holy Ghost and wisdom, whom we may appoint over this business." The witnesses note the appointment of the seven — their election by the church and their qualifications. On the procedure, Barnes: "the right of selecting deacons resides in the church, and does not pertain to the ministry... Whom we may appoint — though they were selected by the church, yet the power of ordaining them was retained by the apostles. Thus the rights of both were preserved." The church chooses, the apostles appoint.

The grammatical voices note the origin and the qualifications. Lightfoot: "This office of the deacon was translated from the Jewish to the Christian church. For there belonged to every synagogue three deacons" (the parnasim). On the qualifications, Barnes: "men who were eminently under the influence of the Holy Ghost, or who were of distinguished piety... wisdom, prudence, or skill, to make a wise and equable distribution." Even the administration of charity required spiritual men.

The Reformed Calvin draws out the balance of church government: "this is the mean between tyranny and confused liberty, that nothing be done without the consent and approbation of the people, yet so that the pastors moderate and govern... that their authority may be as a bridle to keep under the people." And the care in choosing: "we must use great circumspection that we choose none unto the holy function of the Church unless we have some trial of him first."

The witnesses agree: the apostles direct the church to choose seven men of good report, full of the Holy Spirit and wisdom, whom they would appoint over the administration of charity. The procedure preserves the rights of both church and ministry: the people elect, the apostles ordain (Barnes) — the mean between tyranny and confused liberty (Calvin). The office, drawn from the Jewish synagogue's parnasim (Lightfoot), required not merely honesty but spiritual men full of the Spirit and wisdom, for even the care of the poor demands grace and prudence. The number seven was suited to the need, with no mystery intended. The verse displays the order and wisdom of the early church: a charity administered by chosen, spiritual, prudent men, elected by the people and confirmed by the apostles. It models a church government that is neither autocratic nor disorderly, and a standard for office that requires both character and competence. The relief of the poor is no mean task, but one demanding men of the Spirit.
4:
"But we will give ourselves continually to prayer, and to the ministry of the word." The witnesses note the apostles' declaration of their proper work. The grammatical voices stress the intensity of the commitment. Barnes: "The original expression denotes intense and persevering application to a thing... they meant to make this their constant and main object, undistracted by the cares of life." Robertson notes the same verb (diakonia) for the deacons' "ministration" (v.1) is here used of "the ministry of the word" — the apostles' special service.

On the two parts — prayer and preaching — Ellicott: "These formed the true work of the Apostles... Prayer includes the public worship of the Church in all its various developments, as well as private prayer and intercession; the ministry of the word, all forms of teaching." Wesley: "This is doubtless the proper business of a Christian bishop: to speak to God in prayer; to men in preaching his word."

The Reformed Calvin draws out why prayer is joined to preaching: "they adjoin thereunto prayer, not that they alone ought to pray... but because they have peculiar causes to pray above all others... we shall lose all our labour bestowed upon plowing, sowing, and watering, unless the increase come from heaven (1 Corinthians 3:7). Therefore, it shall not suffice to take great pains in teaching, unless we require the blessing at the hands of the Lord."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles declare their proper work — to give themselves continually (with intense, persevering application, Barnes) to prayer and the ministry of the word. The two parts are the true work of the minister: prayer (public and private) and preaching (all forms of teaching, Ellicott, Wesley). Calvin draws out why prayer is joined to preaching: all labour in teaching is vain unless God give the increase, so the minister must seek the Lord's blessing on his word. The verse displays the priority and pattern of the gospel ministry: not administration but prayer and preaching, pursued with undistracted devotion. The apostles, having delegated the care of the poor (vv.3,5), reserve themselves wholly for their primary calling. The verse remains the charter of the ministerial office: to speak to God in prayer and to men in preaching, giving the whole self to these, and seeking from God the increase that no human labour can produce. Prayer and the word are the minister's twin and inseparable duties.
5:
"And the saying pleased the whole multitude: and they chose Stephen, a man full of faith and of the Holy Ghost, and Philip, and Prochorus, and Nicanor, and Timon, and Parmenas, and Nicolas a proselyte of Antioch:" The witnesses note the church's unanimous choice and the names of the seven. Robertson observes: "Each one of the seven has a Greek name and was undoubtedly a Hellenist, not an Aramaean Jew. Consummate wisdom is here displayed for the murmuring had come from the Hellenists, seven of whom were chosen to take proper care of the widows of Hellenists." The choice of all Hellenists wisely settled the grievance.

On Stephen, the witnesses note his preeminence. Gill: "full of faith... he had an uncommon share of grace... a sufficiency for the service and sufferings he was called to." Calvin: "Stephen did excel first in faith, and, secondly, in other virtues; so that it was evident that he had abundance of the grace of the Spirit." He is named first, as obtaining the largest vote (Ellicott), and would be the first martyr.

On Nicolas, a genuine question arises. Barnes: "From this man some of the Fathers say that the sect of the Nicolaitanes (Revelation 2:6,15) took their rise. But the evidence of this is not clear." Calvin and Clarke note the ancient writers affirm it; Calvin: "the ancient writers do, with great consent, affirm that this Nicholas... was an author of a filthy and wicked sect." Yet the identification remains uncertain, and Calvin draws the sober lesson on the care needed in choosing ministers.

The witnesses agree: the church unanimously chose the seven, all bearing Greek names (all Hellenists, wisely chosen to settle the Hellenists' grievance, Robertson). Stephen, named first and full of faith and the Spirit, is the preeminent — the future first martyr, excelling in grace (Gill, Calvin). On Nicolas the proselyte of Antioch, a debate arises: the ancient writers connect him to the Nicolaitan heresy (Calvin, Clarke), though the evidence is uncertain (Barnes). The verse displays the church's wisdom in resolving the discord — choosing representatives of the aggrieved party — and the prominence of Stephen, who would soon shine as the first martyr. Calvin draws the lesson from Nicolas: great care is needed in choosing ministers, for even the apostles' circumspection could be deceived; yet we must not be troubled when, despite all care, we are deceived, but rather humbled and made watchful. The seven, chosen and full of the Spirit, are set apart for the church's service.
6:
"Whom they set before the apostles: and when they had prayed, they laid their hands on them." The witnesses note the ordination of the seven by prayer and the laying on of hands. On the rite, Barnes: "Among the Jews it was customary to lay hands on the head of a person who was set apart to any particular office... not to impart any power or ability, but to designate that they received their authority, or commission." Ellicott: "This is the first mention of the act in the New Testament... Its primary symbolism would seem to be that of the concentration for the moment of all the spiritual energy of prayer upon him on whom men lay their hands; and so of the bestowal of any office for which spiritual gifts are required."

The grammatical voices note the relation of choice and ordination. Clarke draws out the full process: the church chose, the apostles confirmed; "the choice of the Church was not sufficient... but, as they knew their own members best, the apostles directed them to choose... Seven persons being chosen... are presented to the apostles for their approbation and confirmation." Robertson notes the laying on of hands "was of the nature of a prayer that God would bestow the necessary gifts, rather than a pledge that they were actually conferred" (Hackett).

The Reformed Calvin guards the meaning of the rite: "Laying on of hands was a solemn sign of consecration under the law... because this ceremony should of itself be vain, they add thereunto prayer... the laying on of hands is a rite agreeing unto order and comeliness... and yet it hath of itself no force or power, but the effect dependeth upon the Spirit of God alone; which is generally to be thought of all ceremonies."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the seven, chosen by the church, were set before the apostles, who ordained them by prayer and the laying on of hands — the first NT mention of the rite (Ellicott). The laying on of hands signified the commission and consecration to office, and was joined to prayer; it imparted no power of itself but designated the bestowal of office and sought the gifts needed (Barnes, Robertson). Calvin guards the rite: it is a comely sign agreeing with order, but its force depends wholly on the Spirit of God, as with all ceremonies. The verse displays the order of ordination: the church's choice confirmed by the apostles' prayer and imposition of hands. It establishes the pattern of setting apart to office in the church — a solemn act of prayer and consecration, neither an empty form nor a magical conveyance of grace, but a comely sign joined to the prayer that God would bestow what the office requires. The seven are thus solemnly set apart for their service.
7:
"And the word of God increased; and the number of the disciples multiplied in Jerusalem greatly; and a great company of the priests were obedient to the faith." The witnesses note the remarkable growth that followed the wise resolution of the discord — including the conversion of priests. Gill: "This stratagem of Satan did not succeed to divide the church, but issued in the better decorum and discipline of it, and in the spread and success of the Gospel." Robertson: the word "kept on growing all the more because the apostles were now relieved from the daily ministration of the food."

On the conversion of the priests, the witnesses marvel. Barnes: "their conversion was a striking proof of the power of truth. It is probable that they had been opposed to the gospel with quite as much hostility as any other class of the Jews... the gospel was sufficiently mighty to humble even the proud priest to the foot of the cross." Clarke: "that persons so intent on the destruction of Christ... should at last espouse that doctrine, is astonishing."

Ellicott connects the priests' conversion to Stephen's new teaching: "The new fact may fairly be connected with the new teaching of Stephen... that the time for sacrifices had passed away, and that the Law, as a whole, and the ritual of the Temple in particular, were decaying... To them, it may well have been, that daily round of a ritual of slaughtered victims had become unspeakably wearisome." The priests, weary of profitless ritual, were drawn to the higher truth.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the wise resolution of the discord (vv.2-6) issued not in division but in great growth — the word increased, the disciples multiplied, and (most remarkably) a great company of priests obeyed the faith. The conversion of the priests, the most hostile class, was a striking proof of the gospel's power to humble even the proud (Barnes, Clarke); and Ellicott connects it to Stephen's new teaching of the passing of the temple and its sacrifices, which drew priests weary of profitless ritual. The verse displays the overruling of Satan's stratagem: the discord that threatened the church became, through wise handling, the occasion of fresh increase. The apostles, freed for the word, saw it grow; and even priests were won. The verse marks the triumph of the gospel over both internal discord and external hostility: the church, having settled its first contention, advances mightily, and the very class that had crucified Christ yields to his name. The relief of the poor, well ordered, made room for the spread of the word.
8:
"And Stephen, full of faith and power, did great wonders and miracles among the people." The witnesses note Stephen's emergence as a worker of wonders, beyond his office of charity. Chrysostom: "See how even among the seven one was preeminent, and won the first prize. For though the ordination was common to him and them, yet he drew upon himself greater grace. And observe, how he wrought no signs and wonders before this time, but only when he became publicly known; to show that grace alone is not sufficient, but there must be ordination also; so that there was a further access of the Spirit."

The puritan Henry notes Stephen's faithfulness and elevation: "being faithful in a little, he was entrusted with more; and, though we do not find him propagating the gospel by preaching and baptizing, yet we find him here called out to very honourable services... He was full of faith and power, that is, of a strong faith, by which he was enabled to do great things. Those that are full of faith are full of power, because by faith the power of God is engaged for us."

On the variant reading, Gill notes "full of grace" (the Alexandrian copy, the Vulgate, Syriac): "he had an uncommon share of it; it was exceeding abundant in him." Stephen, full of faith (or grace) and power, did great wonders — the deacon called to charity now shining as a worker of miracles and a defender of the faith.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the Stephen narrative. The witnesses agree: Stephen, full of faith (or grace) and power, did great wonders among the people — emerging, beyond his office of charity, as a preeminent worker of miracles. Chrysostom notes his preeminence among the seven: though sharing the common ordination, he drew greater grace, and wrought wonders only when publicly known — showing that grace and ordination together fitted him for the work. Henry draws the lesson: faithful in the little (charity), he was entrusted with more; and full of faith, he was full of power, for faith engages the power of God. The verse displays Stephen's elevation from servant of tables to worker of wonders and champion of the gospel — the first of the seven to shine, and soon the first martyr. His faith and power, manifest in great miracles, prepared the way for the conflict with the synagogues (v.9) and the testimony that would seal his martyrdom. The deacon of charity becomes the proto-martyr of the church, full of faith, grace, and power.
9:
"Then there arose certain of the synagogue, which is called the synagogue of the Libertines, and Cyrenians, and Alexandrians, and of them of Cilicia and of Asia, disputing with Stephen." The witnesses note the rise of opposition from the Hellenist synagogues. On the "Libertines," the witnesses divide: most (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott) take them as freedmen — Jews once enslaved by Rome (perhaps captives of Pompey), now freed and settled in Jerusalem with their own synagogue; others (Bishop Pearce, cited by Barnes and Clarke) take "Libertines" as the name of a place (Libertina) in Africa. Robertson notes Luke may describe two groups or five synagogues.

The grammatical voices note the special interest of Cilicia. Ellicott: "The young Jew of Tarsus, the disciple of Gamaliel, could not fail to be among the leading members of this section." Robertson: "One of their synagogues had men from Cilicia in it, making it practically certain that young Saul of Tarsus... was present and tried his wits with Stephen. His ignominious defeat may be one explanation of his zest in the stoning of Stephen." The future Paul was likely among Stephen's opponents.

The Reformed Calvin reads the opposition as the beginning of persecution: "This was the beginning of persecution, because the wicked, after that they have essayed in vain to set themselves against Christ by disputing... fly unto slanders, and tumults, and at length they break out into violence and murder." The strangers brought to Jerusalem by God's grace, who should have embraced Christ most readily, instead assault him first.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: opposition to Stephen arose from the Hellenist synagogues — the Libertines (freedmen, or a place-name), Cyrenians, Alexandrians, and men of Cilicia and Asia — who disputed with him. Among the Cilicians, it is practically certain, was Saul of Tarsus (Ellicott, Robertson), whose defeat in argument may have fed his later zeal in Stephen's death. Calvin reads this as the beginning of persecution: the wicked, failing to overcome Christ by disputing, turn to slander, tumult, and at last murder. The verse displays the rising hostility against the gospel, now from the Hellenist Jews who should have been most ready to receive it. The disputation, in which Stephen would prevail (v.10), set the stage for the false witnesses (v.11) and the martyrdom to come. The presence of the future apostle Paul among Stephen's adversaries is one of the providential ironies of the narrative: the man who would become the great champion of the gospel first stood among its persecutors, vanquished in argument by the proto-martyr he would help to slay.
10:
"And they were not able to resist the wisdom and the spirit by which he spake." The witnesses note Stephen's irresistible wisdom, fulfilling Christ's promise. Gill: "they were not a match for him with respect to the knowledge of divine things; they could not answer the wise arguments he made use of, fetched out of the Scriptures of truth." The promise of Luke 21:15 — "I will give you a mouth and wisdom, which all your adversaries shall not be able to gainsay or resist" — was fulfilled in him.

The grammatical voices note the meaning of "wisdom and spirit." Robertson: "They stood up against Stephen's wisdom and the Holy Spirit by whom he spoke." Ellicott marks Stephen's distinction: "It is remarkable that Stephen is the first Christian teacher of whom wisdom is thus specially predicated... wider thoughts, a clearer vision of the truth, the development of what had been before latent in hints and parables." Barnes takes "spirit" as Stephen's zeal and earnestness, which carried conviction beyond mere argument.

The Reformed Calvin draws the comfort for all Christ's servants: "Christ hath promised the same Spirit to all his servants, let us only defend the truth faithfully, and let us crave a mouth and wisdom of him, and we shall be sufficiently furnished to speak, so that neither the wit, neither yet the babbling of our adversaries, shall be able to make us ashamed." He instances the martyrs of his own day, unlearned men who confounded the chief theologians of the Papacy.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Stephen's opponents could not resist the wisdom and the Spirit by which he spoke — the fulfillment of Christ's promise (Luke 21:15) that he would give his witnesses a wisdom none could gainsay. Stephen is the first Christian teacher of whom "wisdom" is specially predicated (Ellicott), marking a clearer vision of the truth; and his irresistible power was both the wisdom of his arguments from Scripture (Gill) and the Spirit by whom he spoke (Robertson). Calvin draws the comfort for all believers: the same Spirit is promised to all Christ's servants, so that the faithful, craving wisdom from him, need not fear the wit of their adversaries — as the unlearned martyrs confounded the learned in Calvin's own day. The verse displays the triumph of the Spirit-given wisdom over human cunning: Stephen, full of the Spirit, overcame all his disputants, who, unable to answer, turned to slander and violence (v.11). The promise of Christ stands: his witnesses, armed with his wisdom and Spirit, cannot be resisted in argument, however the world may resort to force.
11:
"Then they suborned men, which said, We have heard him speak blasphemous words against Moses, and against God." The witnesses note the resort to false witness, when argument failed. Barnes: "To suborn in law means to procure a person to take such a false oath as constitutes perjury... they induced them to declare that which was false, or to bring a false accusation against him. This was done not by declaring a palpable falsehood, but by perverting his doctrines, and by stating their own inferences as what he had actually maintained — the common way in which men oppose doctrines from which they differ."

The grammatical voices note the parallel with Christ's trial. Robertson: "One recalls the plight of Caiaphas in the trial of Jesus when he sought false witnesses... They put these men forward in an underhand way for fraud." The charge of blasphemy against Moses and God was the most deadly that could be brought, and carried the penalty of stoning.

On the nature of the charge, Barnes notes its plausibility: "What was said in the mildest manner, and in the way of cool argument, might easily be perverted so as in their view to amount to blasphemy." Ellicott: "The charge was a false one, but its falsehood was a distortion of the truth, as that against our Lord had been." Stephen had likely taught the passing of the temple and the law, which they twisted into blasphemy.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: when they could not resist Stephen's wisdom (v.10), they suborned false witnesses to charge him with blasphemy against Moses and God — perverting his actual teaching into the most deadly accusation (Barnes, Ellicott), as Caiaphas had done against Christ (Robertson). The charge was false, yet plausible: Stephen had likely taught the passing of the temple and the ceremonial law, which his enemies twisted into blasphemy against the lawgiver and God. The verse displays the malice of defeated opposition: unable to overcome the truth by argument, they resort to slander and false witness. The resort to perjury, when reason fails, is the mark of a bad cause (Clarke, under v.9). The verse marks the turn from disputation to persecution: Stephen, having confounded his opponents, is now to be destroyed by lies, as his Master was. The false charge of blasphemy, the most damning that could be brought, would bring him to the council and to his death — the proto-martyr following the path of the crucified Lord.
12:
"And they stirred up the people, and the elders, and the scribes, and came upon him, and caught him, and brought him to the council," The witnesses note the inflaming of the masses and the seizing of Stephen. Robertson: "They stirred up the people — they shook the people together like an earthquake... The elders and the scribes (Pharisees) are reached, but no word about the Sadducees. This is the first record of the hostility of the masses against the disciples." The opposition, formerly from the Sadducean priests (chs. 4-5), now comes from the Pharisees and the people.

Clarke notes the alliance of the mob and the authorities: "the Libertines, etc., stirred up the people — raised a mob against him, and, to assist and countenance the mob, got the elders and scribes to conduct it, who thus made themselves one with the basest of the people." The leaders lent their authority to a popular tumult.

The Reformed Calvin marks the progress of malice: "Being overcome with the power of the Spirit, they give over disputing, but they prepare false witnesses, that with false and slanderous reports they may oppress him; whereby it appeareth that they did strive with an evil conscience." The hypocrites, shrouding themselves under zeal, grant themselves leave to slander and seize.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Stephen's enemies, having suborned false witnesses, stirred up the people, elders, and scribes — throwing the masses into commotion (Robertson) — and seized him, bringing him to the council. The opposition has shifted: formerly the Sadducean priests (chs. 4-5), now the Pharisees and the people, who united the authority of the leaders with the violence of a mob (Clarke). This is the first hostility of the masses against the disciples (Robertson), and the alliance of Pharisees and Sadducees, Hebrews and Hellenists, against the new truth. The verse displays the spreading and intensifying of the persecution: from the priests to the whole nation, from threats (ch. 4) to seizure and the prospect of death. Calvin marks the evil conscience of the persecutors, who, overcome in argument, resort to slander and force under the cloak of zeal. The seizing of Stephen and his arraignment before the council set the stage for his great defense (ch. 7) and his martyrdom. The proto-martyr, like his Lord, is dragged before the Sanhedrin by an inflamed people and their leaders.
13:
"And set up false witnesses, which said, This man ceaseth not to speak blasphemous words against this holy place, and the law:" The witnesses note the specific charge against the temple and the law. Barnes addresses why they are called false witnesses, even if they reported his words: "if they did report merely his words... they perverted his meaning. They accused him of blasphemy... That Stephen had spoken in such a manner, or had designed to reproach Moses, there is no evidence." The witnesses were false either in invention or in perversion.

The grammatical voices note the charge's parallel with Christ. Robertson: "False witnesses. Just as Caiaphas did with Jesus. Ceaseth not — wild charge just like a false witness that Stephen talks in the synagogues against the law and the holy temple." The "holy place" is the temple, the "law" the law of Moses — the two pillars of Jewish reverence.

Barnes argues the testimony was wholly false: "It is altogether improbable that he taught that Jesus would abolish the peculiar customs and rites of the Jews. It was long, and after much discussion, before the apostles themselves were convinced of it... The probability therefore is, that the whole testimony was false, and was artfully invented to produce the utmost exasperation among the people, and yet was at the same time so plausible as to be easily believed."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the false witnesses charged Stephen with ceaselessly speaking against "this holy place" (the temple) and "the law" — the two pillars of Jewish reverence. They are rightly called false witnesses, for whether they invented the charge or perverted his actual words, they misrepresented his meaning (Barnes), as Caiaphas's witnesses had done against Christ (Robertson). The charge was artfully framed to exasperate the people, plausible yet false. The verse displays the malice and craft of the persecutors: they attack Stephen on the points most sensitive to the Jews — the temple and the law — twisting his teaching on the passing of the ceremonial system into blasphemy against the sacred institutions. The accusation, like that against Christ, rested on a distortion of words he may have spoken about the temple's coming end. The verse marks the formal indictment that would lead to Stephen's defense (ch. 7) and death: the proto-martyr charged, as his Lord was, with speaking against the temple and the law, the very things he most truly honoured by pointing them to their fulfillment in Christ.
14:
"For we have heard him say, that this Jesus of Nazareth shall destroy this place, and shall change the customs which Moses delivered us." The witnesses note the specific words attributed to Stephen and their root in real sayings. Ellicott: "The accusation rested in part on the words of John 2:19, partly on the prediction of Matthew 24:2, which Stephen must have known, and may well have reproduced. It would seem to the accusers a natural inference that He who had uttered the prediction should be the chief agent in its fulfillment." Robertson: "Curiously like the charge brought against Jesus before Caiaphas... It was inflammable material surely and it was easy to misrepresent and hard to clear up."

The grammatical voices note the "customs" charge. Ellicott: "The words seem to have been used in a half-technical sense as including the whole complex system of the Mosaic law, its ritual, its symbolism, its laws and rules of life, circumcision, the Sabbath, the distinction of clean and unclean meats." The charge was that Christianity would overthrow the whole Mosaic system.

The Reformed Calvin gives an extended treatment: Stephen had truly taught "the abrogating of the law" — but of the ceremonial part only, "set the body against the shadows, and the substance against the figure." The Jews, valuing the external, wrested this into blasphemy: "Stephen was so far from doing any injury to the law and the temple, that he could no way better and more truly praise the same... the excellency of the temple and the profit of the ceremonies consist in this, whilst that they are referred unto Christ as unto their principal pattern." The ceremonies' true perpetuity is their fulfillment in Christ.

The witnesses agree: the false witnesses attributed to Stephen the saying that Jesus would destroy the temple and change the Mosaic customs — words rooted in real sayings (John 2:19; Matthew 24:2) that Stephen may have reproduced, but twisted into a charge (Ellicott, Robertson). The "customs" meant the whole Mosaic system — ritual, circumcision, Sabbath, clean and unclean. Calvin gives the key: Stephen had truly taught the abrogation of the ceremonial law — the shadow yielding to Christ the substance — which was no dishonour to the temple and law but their truest praise, for their excellency lies in pointing to Christ their fulfillment. The Jews, valuing the external, perverted this into blasphemy. The verse displays the deepest issue of Stephen's teaching and his death: the passing of the temporary, shadowy system before the substance, Christ. The charge, plausible because rooted in real teaching, was false in intent; for Stephen honoured the law and temple most truly by referring them to their fulfillment. The proto-martyr dies for the truth that the ceremonies were shadows now fulfilled in Christ — the very truth Paul and Hebrews would later proclaim.
15:
"And all that sat in the council, looking stedfastly on him, saw his face as it had been the face of an angel." The witnesses note the divine attestation of Stephen's innocence in his shining face. The grammatical voices stress the impression of serene confidence. Barnes: "This expression is one evidently denoting that he manifested evidence of sincerity, gravity, fearlessness, confidence in God... the calm serenity and composure which will follow a confident committing of all into his hands. It is not meant that there was anything miraculous in the case of Stephen, but is an expression denoting his calmness, and dignity, and confidence in God."

Others read a real, miraculous brightness. Lightfoot: "God himself by a miracle bears witness to the innocence of this holy man, and shows he had done no wrong to Moses, when he makes his face shine as Moses's had formerly done." Clarke: "the light and power of God which dwelt in his soul shone through his face, and God gave them this proof of the falsity of the testimony." Cyril of Jerusalem: "he was radiant with angelic brightness." The shining face, like Moses' (Exodus 34), attested his innocence against the charge of blaspheming Moses.

Ellicott connects it to Paul's testimony: "We can scarcely be wrong in tracing this description to the impression made at the time on St. Paul, and reported by him to St. Luke... his face was lighted up as by the radiance of a divine brightness... the glow of an ardent zeal and the serenity of a higher wisdom."

The witnesses agree: as the council gazed on Stephen, his face shone "as it had been the face of an angel" — the divine attestation of his innocence. The witnesses divide on whether this was a real, miraculous brightness like Moses' shining face (Exodus 34) — God's own witness that the man charged with blaspheming Moses had done no wrong (Lightfoot, Clarke, Cyril) — or the natural impression of serene confidence and conscious innocence radiating from his countenance (Barnes). Either way, the shining face proclaimed his sincerity and his communion with God, and rebuked the false charge. Ellicott traces the vivid description to Paul, who witnessed it. The verse displays the seal of heaven upon the proto-martyr: as he stood charged with blasphemy against Moses, his face shone like Moses' own, declaring his innocence and the presence of God with him. The angelic countenance, whether miraculous radiance or the glow of serene faith, is God's testimony against the lie — and the prelude to Stephen's great defense (ch. 7) and his vision of the Son of Man (7:56). The martyr, falsely accused, is glorified by God before the eyes of his judges.
